figures.caption: Table 6: ImageNet-1K validation accuracy (Top-1/Top-5). Mean and standard deviation are reported over five independent runs for both heavyweight and lightweight models.
Table 1: Comparison of base models and the StableTTA-enhanced models on the ImageNet-1K dataset: model size, computational cost, and top-1 and top-5 validation accuracy.

Figure 7: Monte Carlo simulation. The conflict probability increases as Var(z) grows. In this simulation, we consider distributions: {z ∼ N (µ, σI) | µ ∈ {(1, 0.9), (1, 0.7), (1, 0.5)}, σ ∈ [0.05, 0.25]}. The solid curves show Monte Carlo estimates of the relationship between σ and P(ŷlogit ̸= ŷhard), while the dashed curves correspond to the theoretical (asymptotic) predictions. The empirical and theoretical results are closely matched.

Table 3: Comparison of aggregation strategies (hard voting, soft voting, and logit averaging) under random data augmentation across different models. All results are derived from our aforementioned experiments using TorchVision pretrained models.

Table 2: Experimental results from prior work, evaluated with top-1 accuracy on the ImageNet-1K validation set. Baseline and TTA values are taken from the original papers, where each reported value (e.g., 71.93 [7]) corresponds to the referenced work.

Figure 3: Superior Efficiency and Accuracy of StableTTA. Comparison of the baseline (blue) and StableTTA (red) across the number of model parameters (left), peak GFLOPs in sequential aggregation mode (middle), and total GFLOPs in parallel aggregation mode (right).

Figure 2: (a) Conflict: Given branch logits {z(i) | i = 1, 2, 3}, probabilities p(i) = softmax(z(i)), and predictions ŷ(i) = argmaxp(i), different aggregation strategies may yield inconsistent results. Here, logit averaging predicts ŷlogit = 1, soft voting predicts ŷsoft = 2, and hard voting predicts ŷhard = 3. (a) Explanation: When logits (z(1), z(2), . . .) are sparsely distributed, the conflict ŷlogit ̸= ŷsoft ̸= ŷhard are more likely to occur due to the nonlinearity and non-bijectivity of the softmax function. In contrast, when the logits are more densely clustered, such conflicts are less likely.

Table 4: Ablation studies of StableTTA versus standard TTA under varying numbers of experts (N ) across multiple architectures. StableTTA consistently outperforms TTA under MixUp and CutMix data augmentations.

Figure 5: (a) Baseline: a single model with one forward pass. (b) Multi-model ensemble: multiple models with multiple forward passes. (c) TTA: a single model with multiple forward passes using augmented inputs. Both the multi-model ensemble and TTA aggregate outputs across forward passes.
Table 5: Sensitivity analysis of StableTTA over K and N . Results show robustness to K, consistent gains with larger N , and significant drops when logit processing is disabled (K = C).

Figure 6: Empirical cumulative distribution functions (ECDFs) of p-values from Jarque–Bera tests across models on ImageNet-1K validation set. Each model outputs 50,000×1,000 groups from augmented images, with one test per group. The x-axis shows the significance level α, and the y-axis shows the proportion of p-values ≤ α. The red bar marks the case of α = 0.05. A lower proportion (e.g., 22% of the tested groups under AlexNet) indicates that the majority of groups fail to reject the null hypothesis, suggesting stronger consistency with a normal distribution.
